fields_preview: An {{c1::arterialized}} venous waveform in a vein suggests {{c2::an arteriovenous fistula}}. |

Proximal to an AV fistula, the feeding artery waveform may show {{c1::high-flow, low-resistance arterial flow with increased antegrade diastolic flow}} rather than the expected {{c2::high-resistance}} peripheral arterial waveform. |
Distal to an AV fistula, the native artery may show {{c1::retrograde diastolic flow}} due to {{c2::steal into the low-resistance fistula}}. |
A significant postprocedural AV fistula may be managed with {{c1::a covered stent}}. |
A small, recent, asymptomatic postprocedural AV fistula may be managed with {{c1::conservative follow-up}}. |

Direct Doppler criteria for renal artery stenosis include PSV {{c1::greater than 200}} cm/s, {{c4::renal artery-to-aorta}} ratio {{c2::greater than 3.5:1}}, and {{c3::spectral broadening}} from turbulence. |
Indirect downstream findings of renal artery stenosis include {{c1::tardus parvus waveform}}, {{c2::delayed systolic acceleration}}, and/or {{c3::post-stenotic low-resistance waveform}}. |
{{c2::Delayed systolic acceleration}} in renal artery stenosis means acceleration time is {{c1::greater than 70}} milliseconds. |
In renal artery fibromuscular dysplasia, stenoses are often {{c1::not ostial}}, so PSV may be normal at the sampled location despite downstream {{c2::tardus parvus / delayed acceleration}}. |
{{c2::Echogenic}} material on ultrasound in the main portal vein indicates {{c1::portal vein thrombus}}. |
Untreated chronic portal vein thrombosis can progress to {{c1::cavernous transformation}}, with a shrunken portal vein and collateral cluster. |
Do not call portal hypertension based on portal vein size until the portal vein is {{c1::greater than 1.5 cm}}. |
If the apparent portal vein is {{c1::less than 7}} millimeters, question whether it is truly the portal vein and consider {{c2::cavernous transformation}}. |
{{c2::Doppler flow}} within portal vein thrombus helps distinguish {{c1::tumor thrombus}} from {{c1::bland thrombus}}. |
Complete reversal of flow in the vertebral artery indicates {{c1::severe subclavian steal physiology}}. |
Multiple hyperechoic liver lesions with a hypoechoic halo are suspicious for {{c1::metastases}} rather than {{c1::hemangiomas}}. |
Echogenic liver metastases classically suggest hypervascular metastases, especially {{c1::neuroendocrine/carcinoid, RCC, melanoma, thyroid, and choriocarcinoma}}. |
Melanoma liver metastases may be echogenic because of {{c1::hemorrhage}}. |
Large echogenic vascular cannonball liver metastases should make you think of {{c1::neuroendocrine tumor}}. |

Renal {{c2::cortical}} nephrocalcinosis is classically associated with {{c1::renal cortical necrosis, chronic glomerulonephritis, Alport syndrome, and oxalosis (both cortical and medullary nephrocalcinosis)}}. |
A rough ultrasound rule for hepatic steatosis is liver echogenicity about {{c1::40}}% greater than kidney. |
Increased sound beam attenuation, especially {{c2::loss of diaphragm visualization}}, suggests {{c1::severe hepatic steatosis}}. |
The classic ultrasound finding of {{c2::moderate}} hepatic steatosis is {{c1::decreased conspicuity of portal tracts}}. |
Focal fatty sparing appears as a {{c1::wedge-shaped hypoechoic area}} against an echogenic fatty liver. |
Multiple intrahepatic ductal stones suggest {{c1::recurrent pyogenic cholangitis (but keep Caroli disease in mind)}}. |
{{c2::Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis}} was formerly called {{c1::Oriental cholangiohepatitis/cholangitis}} |
Diffuse echogenicity of a transplant kidney differential includes {{c1::rejection, ATN, calcineurin inhibitor toxicity, and renal vein thrombosis.}} |
Per Dr. Lockhart, increased transplant renal resistive index cutoff is {{c1::0.75}}. |
An testicular/epididymal epidermoid cyst is treated by {{c1::enucleation}}. |
What is a common symptom experienced with a sperm granuloma? {{c1::Pain}} |
Sarcoid testicular involvement can appear as {{c1::multiple bilateral 2-3 mm hypoechoic nodules}}. |
The most common congenital adrenal hyperplasia enzyme deficiency is {{c1::21-hydroxylase deficiency}}. |
Cutaneous/subcutaneous echogenic shadowing foci on ultrasound suggest {{c1::calcinosis cutis, classically associated with dermatomyositis or systemic sclerosis/scleroderma}}. |
A relatively homogeneous hypervascular solid hypoechoic testicular mass in a man in his 20s favors {{c1::seminoma}}. |
A hypervascular solid hypoechoic testicular mass in an older man favors {{c1::lymphoma or metastases}}. |
Epididymo-orchitis is usually from {{c1::ascending infection}}. |
In epididymo-orchitis, the {{c1::epididymis}} is affected first. |
About {{c1::20}}% of epididymitis cases have associated orchitis. |
Isolated orchitis without epididymitis suggests {{c1::mumps or TB}}. |
Testicular torsion salvage is about {{c1::80}}% if treated within {{c2::6 hours}}. |
Testicular torsion salvage is about {{c1::20}}% if treated after {{c2::12 hours}}. |
Ultrasound clue for {{c2::horseshoe kidney}}: kidney tissue seen {{c1::anterior to the aorta}}. |
{{c3::Deflux}} injection is used in children with {{c1::vesicoureteral reflux to bulk up the UVJ and prevent reflux}}. |
A chronically torsed nonviable ovary can mimic {{c1::hemorrhagic cyst or cystadenoma}}. |
Per Dr. Lockhart, the postmenopausal without bleeding endometrial thickness cutoff is {{c1::12 mm}}. |
Per Dr. Lockhart, the postmenopausal on tamoxifen without bleeding endometrial thickness cutoff is {{c1::8 mm}}. |
Per Dr. Lockhart, the premenopausal endometrial thickness upper limit is {{c1::15 mm}}. |
Junctional zone thickness {{c1::greater than or equal to 12 mm}} supports adenomyosis. |
{{c1::T1 hyperintensity}} is the key MRI finding that suggests a dilated fallopian tube represents {{c2::hematosalpinx}}. |
{{c1::Inflammatory change and diffusion restriction}} are the key MRI findings that suggest a dilated fallopian tube represents {{c2::pyosalpinx}}. |
Parathyroid adenoma differential on ultrasound includes {{c1::thyroid nodule, lymph node, and parathyroid carcinoma if very large/lobular/infiltrative}}. |
Parathyroid adenoma demonstrates {{c1::hypoechogenicity, no fatty hilum, prominent vascularity, and a polar feeding vessel::what key findings on ultrasound?}} |
Do not call small bowel intussusception transient if there is {{c1::obstruction}}, a {{c2::visible lead point}}, or length {{c3::>5 cm}}. |
{{c2::Fibrofatty proliferation}} around chronically inflamed bowel can {{c1::displace other loops}}, making the diseased loop more conspicuous. |
Numerous tiny outpouchings only along the mesenteric border at the splenic flexure suggest {{c1::traction/retraction diverticula}}. |
Transition at the splenic flexure raises concern for {{c1::ischemia}} because it is a {{c2::watershed zone}}. |
Benign ulcer clue on fluoroscopy: folds go {{c1::directly to the crater/surface}}. |
Bowler hat diverticulum: a coated {{c1::neck}} plus coated {{c2::outer surface}} can mimic a polyp. |
Fasting for more than 24 hours before HIDA can {{c1::concentrate gallbladder bile and prevent radiotracer entry, causing false-positive gallbladder nonvisualization.}} |
Despite being an inhaled gas tracer, {{c2::Xenon-133}} can demonstrate hepatic activity in patients with {{c1::hepatic steatosis}}. |
In thyroid tracer handling, {{c1::trapping}} means {{c2::thyroid follicular cells pull tracer into the cell through the sodium-iodide symporter}}. |
In thyroid iodine tracer handling, {{c2::organification}} means {{c1::iodine tracer is chemically incorporated into thyroid hormone precursors on thyroglobulin}}. |
{{c3::Organification}} of {{c4::iodine}} on {{c2::thyroglobulin}} eventually forms the thyroid hormones {{c1::T3 and T4}}. |
{{c3::Tc-99m pertechnetate}} is taken up by thyroid tissue because it is {{c1::trapped by the sodium-iodide symporter like iodide}}, but it is {{c2::not organified into thyroid hormone}}. |
Among Tc-99m pertechnetate, I-123 sodium iodide, and I-131 sodium iodide, the tracer(s) that is/are not organified by thyroid follicular cells is {{c1::Tc-99m pertechnetate}}. |
Among a dose calibrator, well counter, thyroid uptake probe, and gamma camera, the only gas-filled ionization chamber is the {{c1::dose calibrator}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used to measure vial or syringe activity before injection is the {{c1::dose calibrator}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used to measure residual syringe activity after injection is the {{c1::dose calibrator}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used for radioactive wipe tests is the {{c1::well counter}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used to count tiny blood, urine, or wipe samples is the {{c1::well counter}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used to measure thyroid radioiodine uptake at the neck is the {{c1::thyroid uptake probe}}. |
In nuclear medicine, the device used to create planar patient images is the {{c1::gamma camera}}. |
The nuclear medicine device that answers the question of how much activity am I about to inject is the {{c1::dose calibrator}}. |
The nuclear medicine device that answers the question of whether a wipe is contaminated is the {{c1::well counter}}. |
The nuclear medicine device that answers the question of what percent radioiodine uptake is in the thyroid is the {{c1::thyroid uptake probe}}. |
The nuclear medicine device that answers the question of where the radiotracer went in the patient is the {{c1::gamma camera}}. |
{{c2::DOTATATE}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::neuroendocrine tumors::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::MIBG}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::pheochromocytoma, paraganglioma, and neuroblastoma::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::PSMA}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::prostate cancer::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::FDG}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::glucose-avid malignancy, infection, and inflammation::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::FES}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::Tc-99m MAA}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::lung perfusion imaging::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::Tc-99m pertechnetate}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::thyroid trapping, Meckel scan, and salivary imaging::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::I-123 and I-131 sodium iodide}} are high-yield tracers to associate with {{c1::thyroid iodine handling::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::Sestamibi}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::parathyroid adenoma and myocardial perfusion imaging::what pathology}}. |
{{c2::Tc-99m PYP}} is the high-yield tracer to associate with {{c1::ATTR (transthyretin) cardiac amyloidosis::what pathology}}. |
Among common nuclear medicine brain tracers, the dopamine transporter tracer used for Parkinsonian syndromes versus essential tremor is {{c1::DaTscan and I-123 ioflupane}}. |
The detector medium in a nuclear medicine dose calibrator is {{c1::pressurized argon gas in an ionization chamber}}. |
The detector medium in a nuclear medicine well counter is a {{c1::NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal}}. |
The detector medium in a nuclear medicine thyroid uptake probe is a {{c1::NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal}}. |
The detector medium in a nuclear medicine gamma camera is a {{c1::NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal}}. |
